review_text: I’ve been using the OnePlus Nord 5 for a few weeks now, and honestly, it’s one of the best all‑round mid‑range phones I’ve tried in a long time. The Snapdragon 8s Gen 3 delivers incredible speed — multitasking and gaming (especially BGMI and COD Mobile) feel super‑smooth thanks to the 144 Hz AMOLED display. The 6.83‑inch screen is bright and crisp, easily visible under sunlight, and the 3000 Hz touch response makes gameplay really sharp and responsive. Battery life is impressive. The massive 6,800 mAh cell easily lasts over a day and a half with moderate use, and 80 W fast charging tops it up in roughly 40 minutes. Temperature control is handled well, and even after extended gaming sessions, the phone stays comfortably warm. The camera performance is solid for the price — the 50 MP main sensor delivers detailed, well‑exposed shots, and the 50 MP selfie camera is among the best in this segment with sharp, lively images even in low light. The 8 MP ultra‑wide lens is acceptable but could use some improvement in low‑light and edge detail. Video recording at 4K 60 fps on both front and rear cameras is a pleasant surprise. Design‑wise, the Nord 5 feels premium with its matte‑glass back. It’s slightly on the heavier side, but the build feels durable and premium in hand. OxygenOS is still one of the cleanest and smoothest Android skins around, with plenty of thoughtful AI features and minimal bloat ware. If I had to nit‑pick, the phone isn’t fully waterproof (IP65 only) and the cameras, while good, don’t quite match flagship levels. But for under ₹30,000, it’s hard to complain.Pros:Blazing fast Snapdragon 8s Gen 3 performanceSmooth 144 Hz AMOLED displayStrong battery life + 80 W fast chargingExcellent selfie camera and 4K videoClean, fast OxygenOS experienceCons:Slightly heavy and bulkyUltra‑wide camera underperforms in low lightOnly IP65 water resistanceVerdict:If you want a phone that’s built for speed, gorgeous to use, and easily lasts a full day or more, the OnePlus Nord 5 is one of the smartest buys right now in the ₹25,000–₹30,000 range

I would like to preface this review by saying that I am a very new user to OnePlus devices, I've used their Nord Buds 2 and I have flicked through their OS on relative's phones some time ago, but beyond that my experience with OnePlus phones are rather non-existent. I also wish to state that I am NOT a gamer, or a mobile gamer to be accurate. In fact I do not use my phone for many things other than using messaging applications, and documents archive. Doom scrolling or playing games on my phone have never been appealing to me, I am also not a heavy picture taker since I do not do many things that can be considered "exciting" in my life.So my most important features in a smartphone are, storage size and networking. Everything else is basically fluff to me. That being said this phone is clearly intended for the "freshman gamer" demographic. Someone who is packing their bags to college or entering the workforce after a long stint at some institution. The industrial design reflects the need to be discreet but capable which is something this particular niche of people really like.[Display] - 4/5It is the first thing you notice when you use a smartphone, and hence will occupy the first position. It is also the most important part of the phone, you can have the most stacked specs, but if your screen is bad, your overall experience will be bad. Thankfully, the OnePlus Nord 5 has a good display. I cannot vouch for the color accuracy, my eyes are not trained enough to discern such a specialist requirement, but it looks great from what I can see, and it has HDR or "Ultra HDR" so from testing streaming services on this device, I can say it is an absolute pleasure.It's a 6.83 inch (17.34 cm) at  2800x1272 resolution, something they refer to as "1.5K" which I do not understand, maybe it is alluding to 2K resolution, which is more than enough for the average person. The screen can do upto 144 Hz refresh rate, which is the same refresh rate you would see on professional gaming monitors, the brightness can go upto 1800 nits, which allows for mostly comfortable viewing outside, though under direct sunlight like during high noon, the visibility might not be the best. Overall the display looks very good to me, and I'm sure it will be the same for most of the people that buy this device.[Build] - 3.5/5It is plastic with some versions coming with a glass back. I bought the black version of the phone and it has a sort of glossy matte finish (I know it sounds wrong but you'll get it when you see it, is the best way I can describe it). It is very muted in its design, nobody is going to look at this phone and gasp. The design is not loud by any metrics, it looks average, maybe even less than average, nobody is going to rob you after seeing this phone. It can effortlessly fit into an office space, or a student's backpack, and it looks different enough compared to an uncle's smartphone that it won't embarrass you in front of your boys during your gaming sessions.[Software/UI] - 4/5This comes with the proprietary OnePlus OxygenOS which is one of the cleanest android OS versions on the market outside the Pixel Android OS and recently the Nothing phones (though from what I understand the Nothing OS or whatever it is called is more geared for minimalism than giving a stock android experience). There are some OnePlus apps preinstalled on the device like the myriad of AI tools from OnePlus, but you can uninstall them with ease, and there is also the IR remote app which is useful for utilizing the IR Blaster on the device.The OxygenOS from my memory and the whole reason OnePlus devices became as popular as they did, was notorious for being easily tinkered with by developers and enthusiasts. Something about the phone being able to recover from being bricked after you've messed around with it's software or something, I don't remember all the details. It's not that free in its present form, but the software allows for a myriad of customizations, and it is very clean to use so there's very little convolution when you try to change something like, like in other manufacturers.The Nord 5 also comes with a dedicated "plus" button on the left side top which replaces the supposedly "iconic" sound profile slider. Personally I found the plus button to be a pleasant addition as you can program it do a bunch of stuff like take a screenshot, set your device to DND mode, or access the OnePlus AI feature called "MindSpace" which take a screenshot and then summarizes the information through AI into a short note (you can check how it works on YouTube, the system is pretty nice).The MindSpace option brings be to the main gimmick of these new OnePlus line of phones. It is the AI, this thing is just loaded up with AI tools. There is literally some AI tool to do everything you have ever been doing as a human being with these devices, to the extent where there are even AI tools built in that can be set up to automatically reply to your messages in messaging apps, and you can even set the tone of the response 💀. Like bro, that is crazy. They are legitimately automating the human part of utilizing a smartphone. There are AI transcribing tools, there's like AI live translation tools built into the calling application, there's AI photo manipulation, AI device management. In fact before you open up the device you can even juice up the AI even further by removing the limiters on how much information it is able to mine from your device.You can also select which AI assistant is programmed into long pressing the power button (which was not an option in my previous Samsung phones which always went to Bixby). This time I set Gemini, and its pretty good all things considered. I can definitely see people becoming more useless without tech in the future because of the AI tools on this device makes your life too easy, I might even go as far as to say it eliminates sentient thought, and the thing is the systems are going to get even better.[Performance] - 4.5/5All right this is the section most people really want to read, and I admit I buried it this deep so you'll get a better understanding of the device around the raw performance, because it is more consequential, as you spend most of your time using your phone for stuff other than utilizing it's maximum power through demanding applications and games. That being said, you need your phone to be capable when it comes to your leisurely activities, and the OnePlus Nord 5 is very capable.The Processor on this Smartphone is the Snapdragon 8s Gen 3 which is a step back from some of it's competitors which use the Snapdragon 8s Gen 4, but good Lord, these people have milked this chipset to its limit. There is not an ounce of performance wasted with this one, and it shows in the gaming tests. The benchmarking results on YouTube and other enthusiast websites might show this phone fall behind its competitors but in real life its keeps up the pace, and arguably for a longer time because of its optimization.In PUBG or BGMI (however you wanna call it, I played it a bit on PC when it was all the rage many years ago, and only installed it on this phone to check the frame rate) I maxed out the setting and was getting 83 fps at the lowest and 90fps on average but this is because the game locks you to that framerate. You can use the "Performance Booster" option in the OnePlus Game Optimization software and get 129-144 fps constantly with the 129 being the lowest and 143-144 fps being the average. This coupled with the 144Hz display means that you are going to be having an actually significant edge over your competitors who even though they may have superior hardware might not have this rigged software that unlocks your fps.On Genshin impact when maxed out with the performance booster mode turned on I was getting around 85-90 fps which for me was very smooth on such a small screen. There since the pixel density on the small screen is so high, even with less pronounced graphics like those in PC games, Genshin and other games like Wuthering Waves also look great. Performance is also similar. Honestly I never made it to the point where I was playing these games constantly because as I have said I am not that big of a mobile gamer, I just downloaded these games to see what they played like on this device so I could write this review for those who were curious, but from starting up the games and playing through them for a bit, even in scenarios where there were a lot of enemies and there were particle explosions everywhere I was able to maintain a solid framerate around 90 fps regardless of which game I was playing.All in all the performance is solid.[Battery] - 4/5Lasts the whole day, even with some gaming on the side, keep adaptative refresh rate on so that the 144 Hz display doesn't drain your battery all the time. Honestly not much to complain on the battery end. The fast charging means you will get enough juice to pull you through the entire day in under half an hour, which is crazy.[Storage] - 3/5By specifications the Nord 5 has a slower storage memory (UFS 3.1) compared to the UFS 4.0 in the previous Nord 4. But in day to day data transfers you wont be seeing a big difference, especially for small files like documents, or pictures, maybe short videos. Though for large files it is noticeable, with some transfers taking more than 30 minutes compared to a phone with UFS 4.0.[Camera] - 5/5 (for me)This phone got a 50 MP main camera which is perfect for my use case which is mostly capturing documentation and other text blocks at very close distances. Pair that with the AI optimization and it takes extremely detailed scans of documents across all lighting modes.Though if you are a normal person who uses their camera for taking pictures of your travels, or your loved ones. Maybe even snag an selfies when you're feeling cute, then this camera get's the job done but not to the extent some other phones do in this price range. SO if you are buying this phone for it's camera functionality then don't, there are better options out there in this price range. Selfie camera is also 50 MP by the way.[Overall Impressions] - 4/5As I have mentioned in the title of this review, this phone is a work horse. Mostly because they have optimized it for gaming under the hood. I am satisfied with the product, it reflects my lifestyle almost perfectly. It get's the job done, even if you are uncertain how it is capable of doing it considering it's build. Though if I were you I would wait for a sale before I buy it, I was short on time after my old phone started tweaking, so I ordered this one. If possible get the variant with the 12 GB of RAM as applications are becoming more and more demanding, the trend doesn't seem to be slowing down.I'll make updates on this review if I encounter any problems.

After using the phone for one month, my overall experience has been quite positive. The camera good, The battery performance is also good and easily lasts through regular daily usage. In terms of performance, the phone runs smoothly and handles tasks efficiently without any lag.However, there is one major concern. After around 30 minutes of continuous use, the phone tends to overheat, which is not ideal and affects the overall experience.I hope this issue will be addressed and resolved in upcoming software updates.

The fast charging means you will get enough juice to pull you through the entire day in under half an hour, which is crazy.[Storage] - 3/5By specifications the Nord 5 has a slower storage memory (UFS 3.1) compared to the UFS 4.0 in the previous Nord 4. But in day to day data transfers you wont be seeing a big difference, especially for small files like documents, or pictures, maybe short videos. Though for large files it is noticeable, with some transfers taking more than 30 minutes compared to a phone with UFS 4.0.[Camera] - 5/5 (for me)This phone got a 50 MP main camera which is perfect for my use case which is mostly capturing documentation and other text blocks at very close distances. Pair that with the AI optimization and it takes extremely detailed scans of documents across all lighting modes.Though if you are a normal person who uses their camera for taking pictures of your travels, or your loved ones. Maybe even snag an selfies when you're feeling cute, then this camera get's the job done but not to the extent some other phones do in this price range. SO if you are buying this phone for it's camera functionality then don't, there are better options out there in this price range. Selfie camera is also 50 MP by the way.[Overall Impressions] - 4/5As I have mentioned in the title of this review, this phone is a work horse. Mostly because they have optimized it for gaming under the hood. I am satisfied with the product, it reflects my lifestyle almost perfectly. It get's the job done, even if you are uncertain how it is capable of doing it considering it's build. Though if I were you I would wait for a sale before I buy it, I was short on time after my old phone started tweaking, so I ordered this one. If possible get the variant with the 12 GB of RAM as applications are becoming more and more demanding, the trend doesn't seem to be slowing down.I'll make updates on this review if I encounter any problems.

I’ve been using the OnePlus Nord 5 for a few weeks now, and honestly, it’s one of the best all‑round mid‑range phones I’ve tried in a long time. The Snapdragon 8s Gen 3 delivers incredible speed — multitasking and gaming (especially BGMI and COD Mobile) feel super‑smooth thanks to the 144 Hz AMOLED display. The 6.83‑inch screen is bright and crisp, easily visible under sunlight, and the 3000 Hz touch response makes gameplay really sharp and responsive. Battery life is impressive. The massive 6,800 mAh cell easily lasts over a day and a half with moderate use, and 80 W fast charging tops it up in roughly 40 minutes. Temperature control is handled well, and even after extended gaming sessions, the phone stays comfortably warm. The camera performance is solid for the price — the 50 MP main sensor delivers detailed, well‑exposed shots, and the 50 MP selfie camera is among the best in this segment with sharp, lively images even in low light. The 8 MP ultra‑wide lens is acceptable but could use some improvement in low‑light and edge detail. Video recording at 4K 60 fps on both front and rear cameras is a pleasant surprise. Design‑wise, the Nord 5 feels premium with its matte‑glass back. It’s slightly on the heavier side, but the build feels durable and premium in hand. OxygenOS is still one of the cleanest and smoothest Android skins around, with plenty of thoughtful AI features and minimal bloat ware. If I had to nit‑pick, the phone isn’t fully waterproof (IP65 only) and the cameras, while good, don’t quite match flagship levels. But for under ₹30,000, it’s hard to complain.Pros:Blazing fast Snapdragon 8s Gen 3 performanceSmooth 144 Hz AMOLED displayStrong battery life + 80 W fast chargingExcellent selfie camera and 4K videoClean, fast OxygenOS experienceCons:Slightly heavy and bulkyUltra‑wide camera underperforms in low lightOnly IP65 water resistanceVerdict:If you want a phone that’s built for speed, gorgeous to use, and easily lasts a full day or more, the OnePlus Nord 5 is one of the smartest buys right now in the ₹25,000–₹30,000 range

I would like to preface this review by saying that I am a very new user to OnePlus devices, I've used their Nord Buds 2 and I have flicked through their OS on relative's phones some time ago, but beyond that my experience with OnePlus phones are rather non-existent. I also wish to state that I am NOT a gamer, or a mobile gamer to be accurate. In fact I do not use my phone for many things other than using messaging applications, and documents archive. Doom scrolling or playing games on my phone have never been appealing to me, I am also not a heavy picture taker since I do not do many things that can be considered "exciting" in my life.So my most important features in a smartphone are, storage size and networking. Everything else is basically fluff to me. That being said this phone is clearly intended for the "freshman gamer" demographic. Someone who is packing their bags to college or entering the workforce after a long stint at some institution. The industrial design reflects the need to be discreet but capable which is something this particular niche of people really like.[Display] - 4/5It is the first thing you notice when you use a smartphone, and hence will occupy the first position. It is also the most important part of the phone, you can have the most stacked specs, but if your screen is bad, your overall experience will be bad. Thankfully, the OnePlus Nord 5 has a good display. I cannot vouch for the color accuracy, my eyes are not trained enough to discern such a specialist requirement, but it looks great from what I can see, and it has HDR or "Ultra HDR" so from testing streaming services on this device, I can say it is an absolute pleasure.It's a 6.83 inch (17.34 cm) at  2800x1272 resolution, something they refer to as "1.5K" which I do not understand, maybe it is alluding to 2K resolution, which is more than enough for the average person. The screen can do upto 144 Hz refresh rate, which is the same refresh rate you would see on professional gaming monitors, the brightness can go upto 1800 nits, which allows for mostly comfortable viewing outside, though under direct sunlight like during high noon, the visibility might not be the best. Overall the display looks very good to me, and I'm sure it will be the same for most of the people that buy this device.[Build] - 3.5/5It is plastic with some versions coming with a glass back. I bought the black version of the phone and it has a sort of glossy matte finish (I know it sounds wrong but you'll get it when you see it, is the best way I can describe it). It is very muted in its design, nobody is going to look at this phone and gasp. The design is not loud by any metrics, it looks average, maybe even less than average, nobody is going to rob you after seeing this phone. It can effortlessly fit into an office space, or a student's backpack, and it looks different enough compared to an uncle's smartphone that it won't embarrass you in front of your boys during your gaming sessions.[Software/UI] - 4/5This comes with the proprietary OnePlus OxygenOS which is one of the cleanest android OS versions on the market outside the Pixel Android OS and recently the Nothing phones (though from what I understand the Nothing OS or whatever it is called is more geared for minimalism than giving a stock android experience). There are some OnePlus apps preinstalled on the device like the myriad of AI tools from OnePlus, but you can uninstall them with ease, and there is also the IR remote app which is useful for utilizing the IR Blaster on the device.The OxygenOS from my memory and the whole reason OnePlus devices became as popular as they did, was notorious for being easily tinkered with by developers and enthusiasts. Something about the phone being able to recover from being bricked after you've messed around with it's software or something, I don't remember all the details. It's not that free in its present form, but the software allows for a myriad of customizations, and it is very clean to use so there's very little convolution when you try to change something like, like in other manufacturers.The Nord 5 also comes with a dedicated "plus" button on the left side top which replaces the supposedly "iconic" sound profile slider. Personally I found the plus button to be a pleasant addition as you can program it do a bunch of stuff like take a screenshot, set your device to DND mode, or access the OnePlus AI feature called "MindSpace" which take a screenshot and then summarizes the information through AI into a short note (you can check how it works on YouTube, the system is pretty nice).The MindSpace option brings be to the main gimmick of these new OnePlus line of phones. It is the AI, this thing is just loaded up with AI tools. There is literally some AI tool to do everything you have ever been doing as a human being with these devices, to the extent where there are even AI tools built in that can be set up to automatically reply to your messages in messaging apps, and you can even set the tone of the response 💀. Like bro, that is crazy. They are legitimately automating the human part of utilizing a smartphone. There are AI transcribing tools, there's like AI live translation tools built into the calling application, there's AI photo manipulation, AI device management. In fact before you open up the device you can even juice up the AI even further by removing the limiters on how much information it is able to mine from your device.You can also select which AI assistant is programmed into long pressing the power button (which was not an option in my previous Samsung phones which always went to Bixby). This time I set Gemini, and its pretty good all things considered. I can definitely see people becoming more useless without tech in the future because of the AI tools on this device makes your life too easy, I might even go as far as to say it eliminates sentient thought, and the thing is the systems are going to get even better.[Performance] - 4.5/5All right this is the section most people really want to read, and I admit I buried it this deep so you'll get a better understanding of the device around the raw performance, because it is more consequential, as you spend most of your time using your phone for stuff other than utilizing it's maximum power through demanding applications and games. That being said, you need your phone to be capable when it comes to your leisurely activities, and the OnePlus Nord 5 is very capable.The Processor on this Smartphone is the Snapdragon 8s Gen 3 which is a step back from some of it's competitors which use the Snapdragon 8s Gen 4, but good Lord, these people have milked this chipset to its limit. There is not an ounce of performance wasted with this one, and it shows in the gaming tests. The benchmarking results on YouTube and other enthusiast websites might show this phone fall behind its competitors but in real life its keeps up the pace, and arguably for a longer time because of its optimization.In PUBG or BGMI (however you wanna call it, I played it a bit on PC when it was all the rage many years ago, and only installed it on this phone to check the frame rate) I maxed out the setting and was getting 83 fps at the lowest and 90fps on average but this is because the game locks you to that framerate. You can use the "Performance Booster" option in the OnePlus Game Optimization software and get 129-144 fps constantly with the 129 being the lowest and 143-144 fps being the average. This coupled with the 144Hz display means that you are going to be having an actually significant edge over your competitors who even though they may have superior hardware might not have this rigged software that unlocks your fps.On Genshin impact when maxed out with the performance booster mode turned on I was getting around 85-90 fps which for me was very smooth on such a small screen. There since the pixel density on the small screen is so high, even with less pronounced graphics like those in PC games, Genshin and other games like Wuthering Waves also look great. Performance is also similar. Honestly I never made it to the point where I was playing these games constantly because as I have said I am not that big of a mobile gamer, I just downloaded these games to see what they played like on this device so I could write this review for those who were curious, but from starting up the games and playing through them for a bit, even in scenarios where there were a lot of enemies and there were particle explosions everywhere I was able to maintain a solid framerate around 90 fps regardless of which game I was playing.All in all the performance is solid.[Battery] - 4/5Lasts the whole day, even with some gaming on the side, keep adaptative refresh rate on so that the 144 Hz display doesn't drain your battery all the time. Honestly not much to complain on the battery end. The fast charging means you will get enough juice to pull you through the entire day in under half an hour, which is crazy.[Storage] - 3/5By specifications the Nord 5 has a slower storage memory (UFS 3.1) compared to the UFS 4.0 in the previous Nord 4. But in day to day data transfers you wont be seeing a big difference, especially for small files like documents, or pictures, maybe short videos. Though for large files it is noticeable, with some transfers taking more than 30 minutes compared to a phone with UFS 4.0.[Camera] - 5/5 (for me)This phone got a 50 MP main camera which is perfect for my use case which is mostly capturing documentation and other text blocks at very close distances. Pair that with the AI optimization and it takes extremely detailed scans of documents across all lighting modes.Though if you are a normal person who uses their camera for taking pictures of your travels, or your loved ones. Maybe even snag an selfies when you're feeling cute, then this camera get's the job done but not to the extent some other phones do in this price range. SO if you are buying this phone for it's camera functionality then don't, there are better options out there in this price range. Selfie camera is also 50 MP by the way.[Overall Impressions] - 4/5As I have mentioned in the title of this review, this phone is a work horse. Mostly because they have optimized it for gaming under the hood. I am satisfied with the product, it reflects my lifestyle almost perfectly. It get's the job done, even if you are uncertain how it is capable of doing it considering it's build. Though if I were you I would wait for a sale before I buy it, I was short on time after my old phone started tweaking, so I ordered this one. If possible get the variant with the 12 GB of RAM as applications are becoming more and more demanding, the trend doesn't seem to be slowing down.I'll make updates on this review if I encounter any problems.

I can definitely see people becoming more useless without tech in the future because of the AI tools on this device makes your life too easy, I might even go as far as to say it eliminates sentient thought, and the thing is the systems are going to get even better.[Performance] - 4.5/5All right this is the section most people really want to read, and I admit I buried it this deep so you'll get a better understanding of the device around the raw performance, because it is more consequential, as you spend most of your time using your phone for stuff other than utilizing it's maximum power through demanding applications and games. That being said, you need your phone to be capable when it comes to your leisurely activities, and the OnePlus Nord 5 is very capable.The Processor on this Smartphone is the Snapdragon 8s Gen 3 which is a step back from some of it's competitors which use the Snapdragon 8s Gen 4, but good Lord, these people have milked this chipset to its limit. There is not an ounce of performance wasted with this one, and it shows in the gaming tests. The benchmarking results on YouTube and other enthusiast websites might show this phone fall behind its competitors but in real life its keeps up the pace, and arguably for a longer time because of its optimization.In PUBG or BGMI (however you wanna call it, I played it a bit on PC when it was all the rage many years ago, and only installed it on this phone to check the frame rate) I maxed out the setting and was getting 83 fps at the lowest and 90fps on average but this is because the game locks you to that framerate. You can use the "Performance Booster" option in the OnePlus Game Optimization software and get 129-144 fps constantly with the 129 being the lowest and 143-144 fps being the average. This coupled with the 144Hz display means that you are going to be having an actually significant edge over your competitors who even though they may have superior hardware might not have this rigged software that unlocks your fps.On Genshin impact when maxed out with the performance booster mode turned on I was getting around 85-90 fps which for me was very smooth on such a small screen. There since the pixel density on the small screen is so high, even with less pronounced graphics like those in PC games, Genshin and other games like Wuthering Waves also look great. Performance is also similar. Honestly I never made it to the point where I was playing these games constantly because as I have said I am not that big of a mobile gamer, I just downloaded these games to see what they played like on this device so I could write this review for those who were curious, but from starting up the games and playing through them for a bit, even in scenarios where there were a lot of enemies and there were particle explosions everywhere I was able to maintain a solid framerate around 90 fps regardless of which game I was playing.All in all the performance is solid.[Battery] - 4/5Lasts the whole day, even with some gaming on the side, keep adaptative refresh rate on so that the 144 Hz display doesn't drain your battery all the time. Honestly not much to complain on the battery end. The fast charging means you will get enough juice to pull you through the entire day in under half an hour, which is crazy.[Storage] - 3/5By specifications the Nord 5 has a slower storage memory (UFS 3.1) compared to the UFS 4.0 in the previous Nord 4. But in day to day data transfers you wont be seeing a big difference, especially for small files like documents, or pictures, maybe short videos. Though for large files it is noticeable, with some transfers taking more than 30 minutes compared to a phone with UFS 4.0.[Camera] - 5/5 (for me)This phone got a 50 MP main camera which is perfect for my use case which is mostly capturing documentation and other text blocks at very close distances. Pair that with the AI optimization and it takes extremely detailed scans of documents across all lighting modes.Though if you are a normal person who uses their camera for taking pictures of your travels, or your loved ones. Maybe even snag an selfies when you're feeling cute, then this camera get's the job done but not to the extent some other phones do in this price range. SO if you are buying this phone for it's camera functionality then don't, there are better options out there in this price range. Selfie camera is also 50 MP by the way.[Overall Impressions] - 4/5As I have mentioned in the title of this review, this phone is a work horse. Mostly because they have optimized it for gaming under the hood. I am satisfied with the product, it reflects my lifestyle almost perfectly. It get's the job done, even if you are uncertain how it is capable of doing it considering it's build. Though if I were you I would wait for a sale before I buy it, I was short on time after my old phone started tweaking, so I ordered this one. If possible get the variant with the 12 GB of RAM as applications are becoming more and more demanding, the trend doesn't seem to be slowing down.I'll make updates on this review if I encounter any problems.

I’ve been using the OnePlus Nord 5 for a few weeks now, and honestly, it’s one of the best all‑round mid‑range phones I’ve tried in a long time. The Snapdragon 8s Gen 3 delivers incredible speed — multitasking and gaming (especially BGMI and COD Mobile) feel super‑smooth thanks to the 144 Hz AMOLED display. The 6.83‑inch screen is bright and crisp, easily visible under sunlight, and the 3000 Hz touch response makes gameplay really sharp and responsive. Battery life is impressive. The massive 6,800 mAh cell easily lasts over a day and a half with moderate use, and 80 W fast charging tops it up in roughly 40 minutes. Temperature control is handled well, and even after extended gaming sessions, the phone stays comfortably warm. The camera performance is solid for the price — the 50 MP main sensor delivers detailed, well‑exposed shots, and the 50 MP selfie camera is among the best in this segment with sharp, lively images even in low light. The 8 MP ultra‑wide lens is acceptable but could use some improvement in low‑light and edge detail. Video recording at 4K 60 fps on both front and rear cameras is a pleasant surprise. Design‑wise, the Nord 5 feels premium with its matte‑glass back. It’s slightly on the heavier side, but the build feels durable and premium in hand. OxygenOS is still one of the cleanest and smoothest Android skins around, with plenty of thoughtful AI features and minimal bloat ware. If I had to nit‑pick, the phone isn’t fully waterproof (IP65 only) and the cameras, while good, don’t quite match flagship levels. But for under ₹30,000, it’s hard to complain.Pros:Blazing fast Snapdragon 8s Gen 3 performanceSmooth 144 Hz AMOLED displayStrong battery life + 80 W fast chargingExcellent selfie camera and 4K videoClean, fast OxygenOS experienceCons:Slightly heavy and bulkyUltra‑wide camera underperforms in low lightOnly IP65 water resistanceVerdict:If you want a phone that’s built for speed, gorgeous to use, and easily lasts a full day or more, the OnePlus Nord 5 is one of the smartest buys right now in the ₹25,000–₹30,000 range

I would like to preface this review by saying that I am a very new user to OnePlus devices, I've used their Nord Buds 2 and I have flicked through their OS on relative's phones some time ago, but beyond that my experience with OnePlus phones are rather non-existent. I also wish to state that I am NOT a gamer, or a mobile gamer to be accurate. In fact I do not use my phone for many things other than using messaging applications, and documents archive. Doom scrolling or playing games on my phone have never been appealing to me, I am also not a heavy picture taker since I do not do many things that can be considered "exciting" in my life.So my most important features in a smartphone are, storage size and networking. Everything else is basically fluff to me. That being said this phone is clearly intended for the "freshman gamer" demographic. Someone who is packing their bags to college or entering the workforce after a long stint at some institution. The industrial design reflects the need to be discreet but capable which is something this particular niche of people really like.[Display] - 4/5It is the first thing you notice when you use a smartphone, and hence will occupy the first position. It is also the most important part of the phone, you can have the most stacked specs, but if your screen is bad, your overall experience will be bad. Thankfully, the OnePlus Nord 5 has a good display. I cannot vouch for the color accuracy, my eyes are not trained enough to discern such a specialist requirement, but it looks great from what I can see, and it has HDR or "Ultra HDR" so from testing streaming services on this device, I can say it is an absolute pleasure.It's a 6.83 inch (17.34 cm) at  2800x1272 resolution, something they refer to as "1.5K" which I do not understand, maybe it is alluding to 2K resolution, which is more than enough for the average person. The screen can do upto 144 Hz refresh rate, which is the same refresh rate you would see on professional gaming monitors, the brightness can go upto 1800 nits, which allows for mostly comfortable viewing outside, though under direct sunlight like during high noon, the visibility might not be the best. Overall the display looks very good to me, and I'm sure it will be the same for most of the people that buy this device.[Build] - 3.5/5It is plastic with some versions coming with a glass back. I bought the black version of the phone and it has a sort of glossy matte finish (I know it sounds wrong but you'll get it when you see it, is the best way I can describe it). It is very muted in its design, nobody is going to look at this phone and gasp. The design is not loud by any metrics, it looks average, maybe even less than average, nobody is going to rob you after seeing this phone. It can effortlessly fit into an office space, or a student's backpack, and it looks different enough compared to an uncle's smartphone that it won't embarrass you in front of your boys during your gaming sessions.[Software/UI] - 4/5This comes with the proprietary OnePlus OxygenOS which is one of the cleanest android OS versions on the market outside the Pixel Android OS and recently the Nothing phones (though from what I understand the Nothing OS or whatever it is called is more geared for minimalism than giving a stock android experience). There are some OnePlus apps preinstalled on the device like the myriad of AI tools from OnePlus, but you can uninstall them with ease, and there is also the IR remote app which is useful for utilizing the IR Blaster on the device.The OxygenOS from my memory and the whole reason OnePlus devices became as popular as they did, was notorious for being easily tinkered with by developers and enthusiasts. Something about the phone being able to recover from being bricked after you've messed around with it's software or something, I don't remember all the details. It's not that free in its present form, but the software allows for a myriad of customizations, and it is very clean to use so there's very little convolution when you try to change something like, like in other manufacturers.The Nord 5 also comes with a dedicated "plus" button on the left side top which replaces the supposedly "iconic" sound profile slider. Personally I found the plus button to be a pleasant addition as you can program it do a bunch of stuff like take a screenshot, set your device to DND mode, or access the OnePlus AI feature called "MindSpace" which take a screenshot and then summarizes the information through AI into a short note (you can check how it works on YouTube, the system is pretty nice).The MindSpace option brings be to the main gimmick of these new OnePlus line of phones. It is the AI, this thing is just loaded up with AI tools. There is literally some AI tool to do everything you have ever been doing as a human being with these devices, to the extent where there are even AI tools built in that can be set up to automatically reply to your messages in messaging apps, and you can even set the tone of the response 💀. Like bro, that is crazy. They are legitimately automating the human part of utilizing a smartphone. I recently purchased the Samsung Galaxy M56 5G and here’s my honest review based on everyday use:📱 Design & Display:The phone looks sleek and modern with a slim profile. It has a large 6.7-inch Super AMOLED+ display with 120 Hz refresh rate, which makes videos, games, and scrolling look smooth and vibrant.🔋 Battery & Charging:The 5000 mAh battery easily lasts a whole day with regular use, even with gaming and streaming. It also supports 45 W fast charging, so you can top up quickly when needed.📸 Camera:The triple rear camera setup (with a 50 MP primary sensor) captures good photos in daylight and decent results in low light. The front camera is solid for selfies and video calls too.⚙️ Performance:The phone runs on Exynos 1480 with smooth everyday performance for apps, browsing, and media. However, in heavy gaming and multitasking, it can feel a bit less powerful compared to some rivals in the same price range.👍 Pros:Excellent AMOLED display with smooth refresh rateLong-lasting battery with fast chargingClean and modern One UI software experienceGood camera performance for everyday shots👎 Cons:Performance isn’t the strongest for gamingCan be pricey compared to alternatives with similar specs.Dolby Atmos Option is not available in mobile speaker.📊 Overall Verdict:The Galaxy M56 5G is a well-rounded mid-range smartphone with a great display, excellent battery life, and reliable camera performance. It’s ideal for everyday users, media lovers, and casual gamers. However, if you’re into heavy gaming or want flagship-level performance, you might find it a bit limiting.

I recently purchased the Samsung Galaxy M56 5G and here’s my honest review based on everyday use:📱 Design & Display:The phone looks sleek and modern with a slim profile. It has a large 6.7-inch Super AMOLED+ display with 120 Hz refresh rate, which makes videos, games, and scrolling look smooth and vibrant.🔋 Battery & Charging:The 5000 mAh battery easily lasts a whole day with regular use, even with gaming and streaming. It also supports 45 W fast charging, so you can top up quickly when needed.📸 Camera:The triple rear camera setup (with a 50 MP primary sensor) captures good photos in daylight and decent results in low light. The front camera is solid for selfies and video calls too.⚙️ Performance:The phone runs on Exynos 1480 with smooth everyday performance for apps, browsing, and media. However, in heavy gaming and multitasking, it can feel a bit less powerful compared to some rivals in the same price range.👍 Pros:Excellent AMOLED display with smooth refresh rateLong-lasting battery with fast chargingClean and modern One UI software experienceGood camera performance for everyday shots👎 Cons:Performance isn’t the strongest for gamingCan be pricey compared to alternatives with similar specs.Dolby Atmos Option is not available in mobile speaker.📊 Overall Verdict:The Galaxy M56 5G is a well-rounded mid-range smartphone with a great display, excellent battery life, and reliable camera performance. It’s ideal for everyday users, media lovers, and casual gamers. However, if you’re into heavy gaming or want flagship-level performance, you might find it a bit limiting.

Chowhan Enterprises.My honest experience with Redmi Note 15 5GI’ve been using the Redmi Note 15 5G as my daily driver, and over time it stops feeling like just “another phone” and starts feeling like part of my routine. The moment you pick it up, it feels comfortable and light, yet solid—not slippery, and not bulky in your pocket. I actually prefer using it without a cover because the smooth finish and slim profile make it pleasant to hold, though I do keep it safe since we all know phones can slip.Display that actually mattersThe curved AMOLED screen hasn’t just looked good in pictures—it’s practical. While commuting on bike taxis or Rapido in bright sunlight, I can glance at maps, messages, and notifications without tilting or shading the screen. Videos and reels feel bright and satisfying because of the clarity and color pop, especially when I watch them after work.Everyday performance that doesn’t make you think twiceFor daily tasks like replying on WhatsApp, switching between apps, managing emails, scrolling social media, using UPI apps, and playing music simultaneously, this phone just keeps up. The UI feels responsive, and multitasking feels natural. I don’t have to rearrange my usage or close apps constantly—it just flows.Battery life & chargingOn a typical day filled with mixed use, the battery comfortably lasts until night without anxiety. When I do need a top-up, the fast charging is genuinely handy—just a short break and I’m good to go again.A standout with Bluetooth audioPairing with my Xiaomi Buds 6 was instant and effortless. Call quality feels crisp even while riding with a helmet on, and noise cancellation has been surprisingly effective. For me, this pairing feels like the phone was built to work with those earbuds.The feel of using it every dayThere’s something subtle but emotional about a phone that doesn’t draw attention to itself with stutters or glitches. It just works. Whether I’m juggling messages, calls, music, or videos, it feels like a reliable companion, not a distraction.Overall, this phone gives me everyday comfort, a display I genuinely use outdoors, solid performance for my routines, and a seamless Bluetooth audio experience. It’s not just specs on paper—it’s how it fits into my day, and that’s what makes me glad I chose it.
Good quality, features and Performance. Value for Money. (Unlike Note 13 5g ,worst product)

It's a masterclass in balancing premium aesthetics with rugged durability. Having used it for a week, I am thoroughly impressed by its "Titan Durability"—featuring an IP66 rating and MIL-STD 810H certification—which is rare at this price point. The Curved AMOLED screen is a showstopper. With a staggering 3,200 nits peak brightness, visibility under direct sunlight is flawless. The 120Hz refresh rate makes HyperOS 2 feel incredibly fluid, while the slim 7.4mm profile ensures it feels much more expensive than it is.Powered by the Snapdragon 6 Gen 3, it handles daily multitasking and social media with ease. The star of the show, however, is the 108MP OIS main camera. Daylight shots are crisp with natural colors, and the OIS significantly improves 4K video stability and low-light performance. The 5,520mAh battery easily lasts 1.5 days, and the 45W charging gets you back to full quickly. While it’s not a heavy gaming machine, it is the perfect all-rounder.

I’ve been using the Redmi Note 15 for a while now, and I’m extremely satisfied with the performance and overall experience. The design looks premium and feels solid in hand. The display is bright, sharp, and perfect for watching videos or scrolling on social media.Performance-wise, the phone is smooth and responsive, even with multitasking and regular gaming. The battery life is impressive and easily lasts a full day with heavy use. Charging is fast and convenient.The camera quality is surprisingly good for the price segment. Photos come out clear with good color accuracy, and the night mode works well. Call quality and network reception are also excellent.Overall, the Redmi Note 15 offers great features at a very reasonable price. Definitely a 5-star product and a great choice for anyone looking for a reliable smartphone.

I purchased the Boat Airdopes Alpha (Swedish White), and I’m genuinely impressed. The Swedish White color looks clean, minimal, and premium — it really stands out compared to typical earbuds in this range.Sound quality is solid for the price. The bass is punchy and satisfying without overpowering vocals, making it great for music, calls, and daily use. The compact case design is another big plus — lightweight, easy to carry, and feels sturdy. The overall build quality definitely exceeds expectations at this price point.What truly impressed me was the customer support. When I faced an issue, the replacement process was smooth and prompt. Not only was the product replaced quickly, but I also received a wired earphone as a complimentary gesture. That level of service really builds trust.Overall, great design, strong bass, compact build, and reliable support — excellent value for money. Highly recommended.
Nice product I get this in only 700rsBuy its quality is not upto mark but it is good on its price

Bought it Yesterday.  I like the finish but it doesn’t not feel like premium.Even 250 wala AirPods from Chor bazar feels more premium like the lid seems a bit loose not sturdy. The sound is ok and think it does not have noise cancellation, charging is ok, But it does not show one of battery percentage either for the case or the earphone it only shows percent but I can’t determine which one is it showing

"I’m genuinely impressed with these earbuds! The sound quality is crystal clear with deep bass, making music and calls extremely enjoyable. The Bluetooth connectivity is fast and stable, and it pairs instantly with my device. The battery life is excellent and easily lasts through the day. The design is sleek, lightweight, and very comfortable to wear for long periods. Honestly, it feels just like premium earbuds at a much better price. Highly recommended!"
The boAt Airdopes Alpha excels at the absolute basics. They are quite decent  if you just want a cheap, reliable pair of everyday earbuds with great battery life and overall good music experience.
A good product overall. Battery is very long lasting, sound is pretty clear, bass is kinda mid but in general, worth the money.
Doesn't work properly...stops playing inbetween..not happy with the product.

The sound quality is upto par at this price range. But sometimes while placing the pods in the case, it won't get disconnected from the mobile/tablet and fails to charge. But it gets fixed once you remove and place it again. Otherwise it's agood product

Great Value for Money – boAt Airdopes 219I’ve been using the boAt Airdopes 219 for a few weeks now and I’m really impressed with the performance at this price range. The sound quality is clear with good bass, which makes music and videos enjoyable. Bluetooth connectivity is fast and stable, and pairing was very easy.The earbuds are lightweight and comfortable even for long usage. Battery backup is also quite good , I can use them for several hours without worrying about charging. The touch controls work smoothly, and call quality is decent for daily use.Overall, this is a great budget-friendly option for anyone looking for stylish and reliable wireless earbuds. Definitely worth buying!

Not that good, I've used many boat products since last 9 years. And this is the first boat product that actually I didn't like. Because the connection gets week while driving a bike and if phone is around 10 steps away. This is the only issue I am facing right now. Used this one for more than 3 months I believe and still in great shape. And yes I noticed latency while playing bgmi aswell. Apart form the above sound quality and built quality is good and yes I dropped this like 3 to 4 times and I was I like its over but not, great built quality.

I purchased boAt earbuds, but unfortunately my experience has been very disappointing. Please find my feedback below:There is no proper indication to know whether the earbuds are fully charged or how much battery percentage is currently available. This makes charging very inconvenient.The total battery life is only around 12 hours. Even if I charge the earbuds in the morning at 8 AM, they completely stop working by around 7–8 PM.The touch sensitivity is very poor. Even a slight accidental touch pauses the music or video. Overall, the touch controls do not work properly.Most importantly, after using the earbuds for some time, the audio starts breaking continuously. Whether I am listening to music or watching videos, the sound keeps cutting off, which makes them very difficult to use.Overall, the product does not perform as expected and needs significant improvement in quality and performance.-VIJAY MISHRA, VADODARA
